title-group.article-title: Unlocking the potential of mesoporous silica nanoparticles: balancing biomedical applications and safety concerns

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) have garnered significant attention across various disciplines, including chemistry, physics, and materials science, owing to their distinctive properties and functionalities. Numerous studies have demonstrated that MSNs possess several advantageous characteristics, such as tunable pore sizes, excellent biocompatibility, and a high specific surface area. These attributes render mesoporous silica nanoparticles promising for diverse applications in medical fields, including in vivo targeting, drug delivery, and disease diagnosis. Nevertheless, recent research has indicated that mesoporous silica may induce cellular and tissue toxicity in humans, necessitating further evaluation of its long-term safety. Additionally, parameters such as the shape, particle size, and surface modification of MSNs require careful control to enhance their biodegradability, regulate the circulation time of nanomaterials within the body, and mitigate the immunogenicity of mesoporous silica, thereby facilitating the clinical translation of mesoporous silica nanoparticles. This article reviews the advancements in research concerning the use of mesoporous silica nanomaterials in targeted therapy, drug delivery, and tissue engineering. This work evaluates the potential applications of mesoporous silica materials in the biomedical sector and delineates future research directions for MSNs by examining and summarizing their biological toxicity and associated risks.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: In recent years, the rapid advancement of materials science, coupled with the enhancement of research methodologies, has significantly expanded the understanding and application of nanotechnology. Nanomaterials have been increasingly utilized across various domains, including medicine (Lin et al. 2022), physics (Cortés-Del Río et al. 2024), and agriculture (Thakkar et al. 2021). This burgeoning interest has prompted a growing number of researchers to explore the potential applications of nanomaterials. Consequently, a wide array of nanomaterials, characterized by diverse properties, structures, and functions, have been investigated and refined to achieve successful applications in different sectors (Wang et al. 2023; Wei-Wen Hsiao et al. 2023; Chu et al. 2024). Extensive animal studies have revealed that inorganic non-metallic materials, such as graphene (Girão et al. 2022), carbon quantum dots (Nasiriani et al. 2024), and mesoporous silica (Maleki et al. 2017), hold significant promise in the biomedical field due to their favorable biocompatibility. Nanomaterials, particularly nanoparticles and layered double hydroxides (LDHs), have demonstrated significant potential in advancing drug delivery systems, as highlighted by several recent studies. Copper carbonate nanoparticles, as biomineralized carriers, exhibit a high drug-loading capacity and pH-responsive release profile, which enables the targeted delivery of therapeutic agents such as glucose oxidase (GOx) and DNAzymes to tumor sites. These nanoparticles cross biological barriers efficiently and provide a controlled drug release upon exposure to acidic environments, enhancing therapeutic outcomes in cancer treatment. Similarly, cobalt hydroxide nanosheets (Co(OH)2 NSs) have shown selective cytotoxicity against cancer cells and inhibited their migration, suggesting a promising approach for metastasis treatment (Lu et al. 2022; Dong et al. 2023; Wu et al. 2024; Luo et al. 2025). Additionally, these 2D nanosheets serve as effective drug delivery vehicles, improving the targeting and delivery of therapeutic agents to cancer cells. In the field of regenerative medicine, LDHs have emerged as versatile drug carriers because of their high drug-loading capacity and tunable release profiles. They offer a robust solution for tissue regeneration, such as in bone, cartilage, and skin repair, by efficiently delivering therapeutic agents in response to specific biological conditions. Furthermore, advancements in tricalcium silicate-based injectable cements, while not directly related to nanomaterials, highlight how materials designed for structural support can also serve as effective drug delivery systems, particularly in regenerative medicine. Together, these studies emphasize the pivotal role of nanomaterials in overcoming traditional drug delivery challenges, enhancing therapeutic precision, and improving treatment efficacy across diverse medical fields, including cancer therapy and tissue regeneration (Lu et al. 2022; Dong et al. 2023; Wu et al. 2024; Luo et al. 2025).

Among these, MSNs have garnered considerable attention owing to their distinctive structure and properties (Li et al. 2024; Weisany et al. 2024; Das et al. 2025). MSNs are primarily synthesized from amorphous, mesoporous silicates, which form a silicate tetrahedral structure stabilized by covalent bonds. The unique structural configuration of MSNs facilitates a high drug loading capacity and a substantial specific surface area. These characteristics render MSNs highly promising for applications in various fields, including drug delivery (Oh et al. 2022), tumor targeting (Liu et al. 2025), and fluorescence sensing (Yuan et al. 2021; Ghafary et al. 2021; Hong et al. 2023; Zhang et al. 2024; Chen et al. 2024). While organic nanodelivery systems such as liposomes (Cheng et al. 2021), exosomes (Nie et al. 2020), and micelles (Baruah et al. 2024) exhibit superior biocompatibility compared to MSNs, they predominantly depend on passive release mechanisms for drug targeting within the circulatory system, which limits their efficacy in precise therapeutic interventions (Abraham et al. 2019; Tenchov et al. 2021). In contrast, MSNs can be functionally modified through alkoxy silane chemistry to produce gate-controlled silica nanoparticles with specific recognition capabilities. This modification, in conjunction with the internal porous delivery platform and external supramolecular recognition effects, enables active targeted delivery, as well as diagnostic and therapeutic applications. Consequently, mesoporous silica nanomaterials demonstrate significant potential for advancing precision treatment in the biomedical field (Wang et al. 2017; Shi et al. 2018; Yan et al. 2019; Bernardos et al. 2019; Li et al. 2022; Ding et al. 2024; Wang et al. 2024; Guo et al. 2025; Liu et al. 2025) (Figure 1).

sec.p: While numerous researchers have highlighted the potential applications of MSNs in the biomedical domain, concerns regarding the cytotoxicity and inflammatory reactivity of MSNs have also been raised by some scholars (Huang et al. 2022; Morais et al. 2022; Tan et al. 2023; Liu et al. 2025). To enhance the gated properties and specific targeting capabilities of nanoparticles during the synthesis of MSNs and their derivative materials, it is essential to attach various superpolymers or even biological macromolecules, such as monoclonal antibodies, to the surface of gated silica particles (Alberti et al. 2015; Nechikkattu et al. 2021). These surface modifications not only improve the targeted delivery efficacy of the materials but also result in varying degrees of increase in their size, particle size, and molecular weight (Huang et al. 2024; Otri et al. 2024; Liao et al. 2024). Additionally, to increase the biomedical efficacy of MSNs, designers frequently employ strategies to extend their circulation time within the circulatory system. However, the human endothelial–macrophage system identifies foreign substances based on their molecular weight (Chen et al. 2015) and particle size (Portugal et al. 2024). The functional modification of the mesoporous silica markedly enhances the immunogenicity of the nano-system, rendering it susceptible to macrophage attack and potentially inducing severe cytokine storms, which can disrupt the body's homeostasis (Parashar et al. 2024; Miao et al. 2024; Zheng et al. 2024). Concurrently, the chemical and surface modification of silica particles can introduce new chemical groups and surface residues, potentially leading to cytotoxicity (Zhao et al. 2021) and tissue toxicity (Dong et al. 2020).

Furthermore, current research lacks tissue-specific investigations of nano-silica materials across various tissues and organs (Memar et al. 2020). Most studies are confined to the cellular level, with limited systematic and comprehensive analyses addressing the risks of biodegradability, cytotoxicity, and tissue toxicity (Braun et al. 2018; Liangjiao et al. 2019; Pang et al. 2021; Solorio-Rodríguez et al. 2021). In light of these gaps, this paper aims to summarize the applications of mesoporous silica in the biomedical field, focusing on drug delivery, tumor treatment, bacterial resistance, tissue repair, and related areas. MSNs also have many limitations that hinder their further clinical transformation, such as their ability to cause cellular and tissue toxicity, biodegradability, and immunoreactivity. This article also summarizes the biological toxicity of nano-silica materials to provide a theoretical basis for future mesoporous silica research.

sec.title: Synthesis and modification of mesoporous silica nanoparticles
sec.p: Porous materials have garnered significant attention from researchers across various disciplines because of their distinctive morphology. These materials are categorized based on pore size into microporous materials, with pores smaller than 2 nm (Ding et al. 2020); mesoporous materials, with pores ranging from 2 to 50 nm (Yang et al. 2019); and macroporous materials, with pores exceeding 50 nm (Table 1) (Rommel et al. 2022; Chen et al. 2023; Li et al. 2024; He et al. 2025). The properties of these porous materials differ according to their pore sizes. Notably, porous silica materials are extensively utilized in the biomedical field owing to their unique physicochemical properties. The synthesis of MSNs typically involves the use of surfactants or copolymer compounds to direct the formation of mesoporous structures. Consequently, residual surfactants often remain on synthesized nanoparticles, resulting in significant biological toxicity. Therefore, developing safer synthesis methods for MSNs is imperative (Mohanan et al. 2024; Kouznetsova et al. 2024; Krishnakumar et al. 2025). In the human body, MSNs tend to aggregate, forming clusters that diminish drug release efficiency and enhance the immunogenicity of the nanosystem, potentially leading to biological toxicity (Carvalho et al. 2022). Consequently, during the synthesis process, it is often necessary to process and modify MSN nanoparticles through techniques such as ultrasound and coating (Shen et al. 2014). These surface modifications may also influence the biological safety of MSNs (Deng et al. 2021).

caption.p: Comparison of main characteristics and defects of materials with different pore sizes.

list-item.p: The small pore size limits the adsorption and reaction of macromolecules.
The synthesis process relies on templates, which makes it difficult to control the uniformity of pores.
There may be issues with template removal.
Macromolecules can be adsorbed or reacted.
Easy to synthesize and template removal is simple.
The specific surface area is small
The pore structure is not as stable as micropore and mesopore

sec.p: In 1992, researchers from an oil company synthesized porous organosilicon materials, including MCM-41, which predominantly exhibits a hexagonal arrangement; MCM-50, which primarily features a layered arrangement; and MCM-48, which mainly has a cubic arrangement, using M41S as the surfactant (Oye et al. 2001; Yoon et al. 2002; Veregue et al. 2020; Rahaman et al. 2023). The pores of these materials typically range from 2 to 10 nm, classifying them as mesoporous nano-silicon materials. This development has led to increased interest in MSNs among researchers. Many studies have focused on the favorable specific surface area and extensive downstream application potential of MSNs, and they are dedicated to developing various synthesis methods to achieve better monodisperse silica particles (Figure 2) (Nguyen et al. 2021; Pietricola et al. 2021). Among the MSNs, MCM-41, MCM-48, and SBA-15 – synthesized by researchers from Santa Barbara – have been extensively utilized in biomedical research (Gutiérrez-Climente et al. 2021). Consequently, this discussion will primarily focus on the synthesis methodologies of these three types of MSNs to provide a comprehensive overview (Sant'Anna et al. 2020; Tzankova et al. 2021; Vaschetti et al. 2022).

caption.p: Synthesis and characterization of mesoporous silica nanoparticles. (A) Synthesis and modification of C-terminal-mediated mesoporous silica nanoparticles from human ferritin heavy chains. Reprinted with permission from Nguyen et al. (2021). Copyright 2021 Elsevier. (B) Synthesis and characterization of ordered mesoporous silica immobilized by formate dehydrogenase (FDH). Reprinted with permission from Pietricola et al. (2021). Copyright 2021 Elsevier.

sec.p: Among the currently synthesized MSNs, MCM-41 is indisputably the most prevalent in the biomedical domain. Its synthesis predominantly involves the use of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as the silica source and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide as the surfactant. These components are catalyzed under alkaline conditions at 80 °C, with the reaction products being isolated after several hours. It is common for these reaction products to contain residual surfactants, which often possess certain levels of biological toxicity. Failure to remove these residues can compromise the efficacy of MSNs within biological systems and increase potential biosafety risks (Croissant et al. 2018; Kong et al. 2021). Consequently, the product obtained upon completion of the reaction must be subjected to thermal treatment in a Martensite furnace at 550 °C for several hours or undergo reflux and extraction in an acidic medium to eliminate residual surfactants, thereby yielding the hexagonal porous silicon nanomaterial MCM-41 with a pore size of 2–3 nm (Hua et al. 2022). Nanoparticles are essential for applications such as drug delivery and targeted sensing within biological systems, necessitating smaller nanoparticle sizes and larger pore diameters. Therefore, to enhance the suitability of MCM-41 for biological applications and mitigate potential adverse reactions, precise modulation of the nanoparticle system's particle size and pore dimensions is required. Research indicates that by incorporating micelle swelling agents into the reaction system and adjusting precursor concentrations, it is possible to increase porosity while maintaining a smaller nanoparticle size, thereby enhancing the drug loading capacity of the nanosystem and improving pharmacokinetic parameters (Li et al. 2022; Zhang et al. 2022).

MCM-48, a member of the M41S family, has garnered significant interest from researchers due to its distinctive structure and properties. In comparison to MCM-41, MCM-48 exhibits enhanced drug delivery capabilities and can form a more intricate dual-channel structure. This structural feature facilitates the binding of drug molecules to cellular protein receptors, thereby enhancing the pharmacological efficacy of drugs. Traditionally, the synthesis of MCM-48 involves prolonged high-temperature reactions using cationic–anionic surfactants as templates, resulting in particles with a relatively large size, typically approximately 1 µm​ in size. These particle dimensions are suboptimal for in vivo drug delivery systems, as they may provoke strong inflammatory responses and potentially induce cytokine storms. Recent advancements have been reported in the synthesis of monodisperse spherical MCM-48 particles, ranging from 70 to 500 nm in size, through a modified Stöber reaction conducted at room temperature using the triblock copolymer polyF127 as a surfactant (Kim et al. 2005; Abukhadra et al. 2020; Borcănescu et al. 2023). This reaction significantly alleviates the challenges associated with the synthesis process and reduces the particle size of MSNs, thereby offering promising prospects for the application of MCM-48 in biomedicine. However, the small-particle MCM-48 synthesized via this method has not yet been subjected to extensive long-term biological toxicity assessments, and its interactions with biological systems remain inadequately understood, presenting new challenges (Wang et al. 2014; Shah and Rajput 2019; Solarska-Ściuk and Pruchnik 2025).

In addition to MCM-41 and MCM-48, monodisperse SBA-15 is frequently employed for targeted drug delivery. It is primarily synthesized from polyethylene oxide, polypropylene oxide, and P123 in an acidic medium. Both rod-shaped and spherical SBA-15 particles were successfully produced, resulting in larger pore diameters and enhanced stability compared to MCM-41. Nevertheless, akin to MCM-48, a significant limitation is the relatively large particle size, with the maximum achievable dimension being sub-micron SBA-15 particles, which does not satisfy the criteria for immune evasion. Further optimization of the SBA-15 particle size to the nanometer scale could unlock substantial potential for applications in the biomedical sector (Liang et al. 2024; Petri et al. 2024).

Meanwhile, the amount of charge on the surface of nanoparticles and the number of hydrophobic groups make MSNs prone to aggregate within the body, thus losing their monodisperse nature. They are easily recognized by the body's immune system and may even interact with hemoglobin and deposit in the circulatory system, where they acquire immunogenicity and trigger inflammatory responses. Therefore, while adjusting the particle size and pore size of nanoparticles, it is necessary to subject MSNs to ultrasonic treatment, surface coating, functional modification, etc., to reduce their aggregation and sedimentation in the circulatory system. For instance, Asima Farooq et al. (Farooq et al. 2018). Researchers modified the surface of MSN with a titanium dioxide coating and loaded it with the potent antihypertensive drug sodium nitroprusside to investigate the interaction between this drug delivery carrier and the circulatory system. Through comprehensive research and analysis, it has been determined that MSNs modified with a titanium dioxide coating exhibit enhanced drug delivery capacity and improved dispersion effects. This modification effectively prolongs the retention time of the nanosystem within the circulatory system and increases the efficacy of targeted drug delivery systems (Kirla et al. 2020; Verma et al. 2023).

In addition to classical mesoporous silica nanoparticles, composite materials formed by combining mesoporous silica with other nanomaterials have garnered significant attention because of their excellent drug delivery capabilities and biomedical application potential, gradually becoming a research hotspot in recent years. For instance, mesoporous silica-metal-organic framework (MSN-MOFs) composites demonstrate broad application prospects in the biomedical field, as their superior performance is reflected mainly in their high specific surface area, tunable pore size, and good biocompatibility. These characteristics endow MSN-MOFs with remarkable advantages in drug delivery, imaging, and therapy. Firstly, the high specific surface area and controllable pore size of MSN-MOFs enable effective drug loading and release, thereby enhancing drug bioavailability and therapeutic efficacy (Beg et al. 2017; Lei et al. 2021). Additionally, the structural characteristics of MSN-MOFs make them excel in biomedical imaging, serving as multifunctional imaging probes for various techniques, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and optical imaging (Wang and Gu 2015; Xu et al. 2023). In drug delivery, the porous structure and high drug-loading capacity of MSN-MOFs make them ideal drug carriers. Studies have shown that MSN-MOFs can achieve controlled drug release through pore size adjustment and surface functionalization, thereby improving treatment precision and effectiveness (Jia et al. 2018; Hua et al. 2022). For example, hollow mesoporous silica@MOF composites achieve precise drug release through pH-responsive mechanisms, significantly enhancing their cytotoxicity and therapeutic efficacy (Zou et al. 2017). Moreover, the biocompatibility and biodegradability of MSN-MOFs provide safety assurance for biomedical applications (Lee et al. 2011; Yang and Yang 2020; Singh et al. 2021). In the field of biomedical imaging, the multifunctionality of MSN-MOFs enables simultaneous diagnostic and therapeutic use. By integrating magnetic nanoparticles and fluorescent dyes into MSN-MOFs, multimodal imaging and drug delivery can be combined, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and treatment effectiveness (Zhou et al. 2021; Wang et al. 2023). Additionally, the structural tunability and surface functionalization capabilities of MSN-MOFs allow them to adapt to diverse biomedical scenarios, demonstrating broad application prospects in disease diagnosis, treatment, and tissue engineering (Ma et al. 2019; Wang et al. 2023).

In addition to mesoporous silica-based composites, the application prospects of stimulus-responsive MSNs in drug delivery are also quite promising, particularly in cancer treatment, where significant progress has been made in the research of stimulus-responsive MSNs. Notably, the development of dual-responsive systems for pH and ROS has enabled precise drug release in specific tumor microenvironments, thereby enhancing therapeutic efficacy while reducing toxic side effects on normal cells (Ding et al. 2020; Leng et al. 2024). First, pH-responsive MSNs have important applications in cancer treatment. Given that tumor tissues typically exhibit an acidic microenvironment, pH-responsive MSNs can release drugs selectively at tumor sites while maintaining stability in normal tissues, achieving targeted therapy. For instance, studies have shown that pH-responsive mesoporous silica‒calcium phosphate hybrid nanoparticles can rapidly degrade and release drugs in acidic environments, resulting in high drug encapsulation efficiency and release rates (He et al. 2017). Additionally, pH-responsive polymer-coated MSNs have been proven effective in controlling drug release, exhibiting good biocompatibility and anti-tumor activity in both in vitro and in vivo experiments (Hu et al. 2014; Thirupathi et al. 2023). Second, ROS-responsive MSNs also show great potential in cancer treatment. Owing to the high levels of ROS in tumor cells, ROS-responsive MSNs can achieve rapid drug release within tumor cells. For instance, researchers including Leng and colleagues developed MSNs based on dynamic covalent bonds, achieving sustained drug release by cleaving pH/ROS-sensitive boron ester bonds in tumor microenvironments (Leng et al. 2024). Additionally, Elbedwehy and colleagues discovered that ROS-responsive charge-reversal MSNs were effective for drug delivery in retinal neovascularization, demonstrating excellent biocompatibility and drug release characteristics (Elbedwehy et al. 2024). Finally, MSNs combining pH and ROS dual-response mechanisms exhibited superior performance in drug delivery. These nanoparticles enable synergistic drug release in the acidic and ROS-rich environment of tumor cells, thereby enhancing therapeutic efficacy. For example, the dual-responsive nanosystem developed by Ding and colleagues effectively inhibited tumor growth in both in vitro and in vivo experiments while significantly reducing drug side effects (Ding et al. 2020). These studies indicate that pH/ROS dual-responsive MSNs have broad application prospects in cancer treatment.

sec.title: Application of mesoporous silica nanoparticles in biomedicine
sec.p: In recent years, the rapid advancements in fields such as pharmacy (Waseeq Ur Rehman et al. 2020), chemistry (Ikemoto et al. 2024), and traditional Chinese medicine (Zhong et al. 2022) have led to the extraction, isolation, synthesis, and modification of numerous novel drugs for disease treatment. However, these developments are accompanied by potential risks, including the emergence of bacterial drug resistance (Peng et al. 2025), the heterogeneous evolution of tumor cells (Patel and Yanai 2024), and inadequate cell regeneration in tissue engineering (Vera et al. 2021). Conventional drugs have become increasingly inadequate in addressing these challenges. Consequently, many researchers have shifted their focus towards the development of nanomaterial-based formulations, leveraging the unique physical and chemical properties of nanomaterials for therapeutic applications (Diez-Escudero and Hailer 2021). From a chemical standpoint, nanomaterials can be tailored with diverse functionalities through various synthesis strategies to address the specific requirements of treating different diseases (Wu et al. 2023). Techniques such as coating and surface modification can enhance their solubility and reduce their degree of cytotoxicity, thereby rendering them more suitable for in vivo applications. In this context, mesoporous silica materials have garnered increasing attention from researchers. Presently, they are extensively utilized in drug delivery, tumor treatment, antibacterial applications, and tissue engineering. Consequently, the author will summarize the advancements in the application of mesoporous silica in these domains in the subsequent sections, aiming to provide insights for further development in related fields (Baleizão and Farinha 2015; She et al. 2018; Vallet-Regi and Tamanoi 2018).

sec.title: Application of mesoporous silica nanoparticles in tumor therapy
sec.p: With the exacerbation of environmental pollution and the increasing pressures of modern life, the global incidence of malignant tumors is on the rise, becoming a significant factor adversely affecting human health and well-being (Liu and Sun 2021; Chen and Song 2022). Consequently, numerous researchers have endeavored to develop chemokinetic drugs capable of treating these malignancies. However, during tumorigenesis and progression, tumors frequently undergo evolutionary changes, resulting in the acquisition of drug resistance or even the development of multidrug resistance (Friedmann Angeli et al. 2019; Boumahdi and de Sauvage 2020; Guo et al. 2024). This has significantly impeded advancements in the treatment of malignant tumors. Nonetheless, with the advancement of interdisciplinary research, some scholars have shown that nanomaterials, particularly MSNs, offer promising therapeutic avenues for treating malignant tumors through mechanisms such as drug delivery and enhancement of tumor immunity, thereby providing novel strategies for cancer treatment (Wang et al. 2023).

Mesoporous silica nanoparticles consist of an amorphous silica matrix, with particle sizes typically ranging from 2 to 50 nm. They possess a surface area of up to 1500 m²/g and a pore volume of approximately 1 cm³/g, which confer a high drug loading capacity. Drugs are released through the efficient pores present on the surface of MSNs, allowing targeted delivery specifically to the high-pressure blood vessels associated with malignant tumors (Nguyen et al. 2019; Lérida-Viso et al. 2023; Xiang et al. 2025). Furthermore, MSNs can undergo surface chemical modifications to produce nanoparticles that are more suited to biomedical applications. Consequently, the utilization of MSNs as drug delivery carriers for the treatment of malignant tumors has garnered significant attention among researchers (Figure 3) (Li et al. 2017,2022; Huang et al. 2023; Li et al. 2024). For instance, Lei He et al. (He et al. 2023) employed MSNs as drug-targeting carriers by encapsulating selenium compounds within them. The introduction of numerous tetrasulfide bond structures into these MSNs significantly enhanced the degradability of the silica matrix, facilitating their decomposition by intracellular glutathione. The nanosystem is designed to be internalized by the elevated levels of glutathione or reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate present in the intracellular environment of tumor cells. This internalization leads to the decomposition of the drug delivery system, thereby promoting the release of selenium compounds. Consequently, this process facilitates the generation of a substantial amount of oxygen-free radicals within the tumor cells. These radicals induce structural damage to critical cellular components such as the cell membrane and nucleus in osteosarcoma cells, ultimately promoting apoptosis (He et al. 2023).

caption.p: Application of mesoporous silica nanoparticles in the treatment of malignant tumors. (A) A novel mesoporous silica nanoparticles synergistically eradicate tumors by amplifying the therapeutic effects of ionic interference through an osmotic surge and enhanced oxidative stress. Reprinted with permission from Li et al. (2022). Copyright 2022 American Chemical Society. (B) Synthesis of a novel hollow mesoporous silica composite nanoplatform for targeted therapy of malignant tumors. Reprinted with permission from Li et al. (2017). Copyright 2017 Elsevier. (C) Mesoporous silica nanoparticles drive the tumor immune response through photodynamic therapy combined with the treatment of malignant tumors. Reprinted with permission from Huang et al. (2023). Copyright 2023 American Chemical Society. (D) Research on the multimodal killing of malignant tumors by composite nanoparticles. Reprinted with permission from Li et al. (2024). Copyright 2024 John Wiley and Sons.

sec.p: Furthermore, the nano-delivery system exhibits significant stability in environments lacking glutathione, thereby minimizing damage to normal cells. This experimental study demonstrated that the Se-MSN nanodelivery system possesses a robust ability to target and selectively eradicate malignant tumors. In a related study, Tsou Min-Hsuan et al. utilized diatomite mesoporous silica containing lanthanide particles (dMSN-EuGd) as a drug carrier (Tsou et al. 2023). This nanosystem not only has excellent drug loading capacity but also possesses photodynamic therapy capabilities. Upon exposure to near-infrared light, the system generates reactive oxygen species capable of inducing cytotoxicity in tumor cells. Consequently, this nanosystem can be employed to deliver the chemotherapeutic agent brown algae polysaccharide for the treatment of the human colon cancer cell line HCT116, while concurrently facilitating photodynamic therapy. Experimental findings demonstrated that the survival rate of HCT116 cells was significantly reduced following treatment with the dMSN-EuGd@Fucoidan composite nanosystem. These findings suggest that utilizing MSNs as delivery vehicles, in conjunction with multimodal synergistic approaches, is advantageous for the treatment of malignant tumors (Wang et al. 2019; Gautam et al. 2019; Liang et al. 2024).

In addition to targeted drug delivery and multimodal synergistic tumor eradication, tumor immunotherapy has garnered increasing interest among clinicians (Liu et al. 2024). Unlike chemotherapy and radiotherapy, tumor immunotherapy typically spares non-tumor cells from damage, instead targeting malignant tumors by activating the patient's innate immune system. This approach promotes the activation of cytotoxic T lymphocytes (Ramirez-Valdez et al. 2023), reduces tumor angiogenesis (Jeong et al. 2022), and inhibits the release of tumor-associated cytokines (Xu et al. 2024). In recent years, a substantial body of research on tumor immunity has been published, resulting in the development of numerous innovative tumor immunity strategies, including chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy (Yang et al. 2023; Parikh and Lonial 2023) and immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) (Morad et al. 2021). However, the successful implementation of these strategies in clinical practice remains limited due to challenges such as the current stage of clinical development, economic costs, and safety concerns. In response to these challenges, various novel immune delivery systems, such as exosomes (Arrieta et al. 2023), liposomes (Tang et al. 2022), and nanomaterials (Chen et al. 2023) have been explored for application in tumor immunotherapy.

Among these systems, mesoporous silica has garnered significant attention from researchers, attributed to its efficient delivery capacity, straightforward synthesis process, and large, ordered pores (Furukawa et al. 2023). Yang et al. (2021) synthesized gold nanorods (AuNRs) with precise size control to fit within MSNs, incorporating the immunogenic cell death (ICD) inducer doxorubicin (Dox) within the voids, thereby forming a composite nanocarrier, AuNR@MSN@Dox, for targeting liver cancer cells. This drug delivery system is capable of inducing ICD in tumor cells, leading to the generation of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and tumor-associated antigens (TAAs). These newly synthesized proteins can effectively stimulate the body's immune system, activating cytotoxic T lymphocytes, dendritic cells, and macrophages, thereby facilitating the eradication of tumor cells (Krysko et al. 2012; Ahmed and Tait 2020; Sun et al. 2021). Concurrently, Yang et al. (2021) employed DNA with a hairpin structure as a gating molecule in the formulation AuNR@MSN@Dox. The heat produced by gold nanorods under near-infrared light exposure resulted in the disruption of hydrogen bonds within the hairpin-structured DNA, preventing the DNA molecule from maintaining its hairpin conformation. Consequently, the internally loaded Dox was released at the tumor site. This approach not only enables the synergistic treatment of malignant tumors through the integration of tumor immunotherapy, photothermal therapy, and chemodynamic therapy but also significantly mitigates the side effects of Dox on non-target cells during chemotherapy.

The aforementioned study suggested that post-onset of malignant tumors, the activation of the body's specific immune system can effectively eradicate tumor cells and enhance patient survival outcomes. Oncology, as a branch of clinical medicine, is dedicated to the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of tumors within specific populations, with tertiary prevention playing a critical role. Consequently, numerous researchers have employed MSN as delivery vehicles for macromolecular antigens or immune adjuvants. This nano-vaccine approach stimulates the body's adaptive immune system, facilitating the pre-emptive generation of memory T lymphocytes before tumor development, thereby inhibiting the onset and progression of malignant tumors. For instance, in the study conducted by Cha et al. (2018), researchers employed super-large porous mesoporous silica nanoparticles (XL-MSNs) as targeted delivery vehicles, incorporating Toll-like receptor 9 (TLR9) agonists to activate the body's adaptive immune response, notably through the activation of dendritic cells, thereby enhancing the body's surveillance of malignant tumors. Experimental investigations revealed that in tumor-free mice treated with these nano-vaccines, subsequent tumor challenges resulted in significant prevention of tumor development.

In conclusion, MSNs exhibit significant potential for application and development in the treatment of malignant tumors. They can be functionalized with various drug molecules and biomolecules to effectively target tumors (Phan et al. 2024). In addition to the previously discussed therapies – such as photodynamic therapy, photothermal therapy, and immunotherapy – additional treatment modalities include gene-targeted delivery (Živojević et al. 2021), adjuvant radiotherapy (Chen et al. 2022), and nano-enzyme catalytic therapy (Wang et al. 2019). These therapeutic approaches can leverage the high loading capacity and large, ordered pore structure of MSNs to achieve synergistic effects in the treatment of malignant tumors. Nonetheless, the synthesis of MSNs has yet to reach a level of refinement that addresses all challenges. Furthermore, the diverse hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties of these molecules may alter the physicochemical characteristics of the composite materials when integrated with MSNs. Consequently, its application has become less common. With the ongoing advancement of experimental techniques and research scopes, novel methodologies for the refined synthesis and modification of MSNs nanomaterials are anticipated to be developed, thereby facilitating significant progress in the treatment of malignant tumors (Li et al. 2019; Huang et al. 2020; Vyas et al. 2023).

sec.title: Application of mesoporous silica nanoparticles in bacterial therapy
sec.p: Historically, infectious diseases have posed a substantial threat to human life, health, and quality of life. In response to the myriad of highly contagious bacterial infections, researchers have progressively identified a diverse array of antibiotic drugs, each with distinct targets and mechanisms of action (Zhao et al. 2020; Klein et al. 2020; Osbelt et al. 2024). However, since the 1980s, the discovery and optimization of antibiotic types have encountered a bottleneck, resulting in many bacteria acquiring drug resistance through prolonged genetic mutations and environmental selection, and even leading to the emergence of multidrug-resistant strains (Levy and Marshall 2004; Fang and Schooley 2020). Currently, the primary mechanisms by which bacteria develop drug resistance include the following three mechanisms. Firstly, bacteria produce specific hydrolases or modifying enzymes to alter the chemical structure or properties of antibiotics (Abbas et al. 2024). Secondly, they modify the permeability of their outer membrane to decrease the influx of antibiotics (Rather et al. 2021). Thirdly, efflux pumps located on the cell membrane actively expel antibiotics that have entered the cell (Mohanty et al. 2021). These mechanisms enable bacteria to proliferate extensively within the host, disrupt internal homeostasis, and threaten patient safety. To address this challenge, researchers have developed nanomedicine delivery systems to encapsulate antibiotics, thereby facilitating targeted delivery. This approach aims to prevent the enzymatic degradation of antibiotics, reduce bacterial recognition, and enhance antibiotic accumulation within bacterial cells, ultimately leading to bacterial eradication (Guo et al. 2024).

Nevertheless, the efficacy of bacterial eradication is significantly influenced by the particle size, porosity, pH responsiveness, and surface modification of nanoparticles. Consequently, considerable research efforts have been directed towards the exploration of inorganic nanomaterials (Wu et al. 2019; Zhou et al. 2022; Zhao et al. 2023). In contrast to organic materials, inorganic materials offer advantages such as a straightforward synthesis process, high controllability, and ease of surface modification. Among the diverse range of inorganic nanomaterials, MSNs have demonstrated significant potential for application. Consequently, this section provides a comprehensive overview of the role of MSNs in antibacterial treatment, thereby facilitating their future clinical translation to address bacterial resistance.

MSNs offer significant advantages owing to their ease of modification. The morphology of MSNs can be tailored to produce various shapes, such as spherical (de Paula et al. 2022) and rod-shaped (Borzęcka et al. 2022) shapes, as well as different structural forms, including hollow (Zhang et al. 2022) and core-shell (Li et al. 2023) forms. These diverse configurations impart distinct biological functionalities to MSNs. For instance, MSNs with hollow cavities are particularly adept at accommodating drug molecules and can selectively deliver macromolecular substances, such as proteins and genes, thereby establishing MSNs as an exceptional drug delivery systems (Li et al. 2023; Liu et al. 2025). This characteristics is especially crucial for the advancement of antibacterial nanosystems. Leveraging the superior structural properties of MSNs, antibiotics can be efficiently incorporated into the framework or organized cavities of the material and secured within the drug delivery system through covalent or non-covalent interactions with the MSN surface (Fang and Schooley 2020). This drug delivery system significantly enhances the thermal stability of antibiotics, minimizes adverse effects on healthy tissues and cells, and improves sterilization efficacy (Figure 4) (Braun et al. 2016; Li et al. 2019; Song et al. 2022; Liu et al. 2025; Chen et al. 2025; Nikmanesh et al. 2025).

caption.p: Antimicrobial applications of mesoporous silica nanoparticles. (A) Mesoporous silica nanoparticles for the treatment of Salmonella typhi. Reprinted with permission from Chen et al. (2025). Copyright 2025 American Chemical Society. (B) Effects of surface on the antibacterial action of mesoporous silica nanoparticles. Reprinted with permission from Braun et al. (2016). Copyright 2016 Elsevier. (C) Antibacterial activity was enhanced by spike mesoporous silica nanoparticles synthesized via the double-template catalytic method. Reprinted with permission from Song et al. (2022). Copyright 2022 Springer Nature. (D) The synthesis of novel covalently modified mesoporous silica nanoparticles and their bactericidal effect by photodynamic therapy. Reprinted with permission from Liu et al. (2025). Copyright 2025 John Wiley and Sons.

sec.p: For instance, researchers such as Tabriz et al. (2023) have employed the sol–gel method, utilizing CTAB and TEOS as precursors, to introduce silver sites and chitosan into MSN for the synthesis of a nano-drug delivery system. This system was subsequently loaded with the broad-spectrum antibacterial agents meropenem and imipenem, resulting in the formation of meropenem-imipenem/chitosan/Ag-MSN/NH2 (MCAM). Studies have demonstrated that MCAM can effectively penetrate the outer and cell membranes of bacteria, thereby overcoming bacterial drug resistance barriers. The silver incorporated within the nano-drug delivery system facilitates the generation of reactive oxygen species and free radicals within bacterial cells, leading to damage to genetic material and organelle membranes. This action synergistically enhances the bactericidal effects of meropenem-imipenem, particularly against Gram-negative bacteria (Tabriz et al. 2023).

Additionally, researchers such as Aguilera-Correa et al. (2022) synthesized gelatin and polymyxin-coated MSNs, using these materials as surface modification coatings for MCM-41. The coatings were employed to improve the biocompatibility and stability of mesoporous silica while concurrently facilitating the dissolution of bacterial biofilms. The broad-spectrum antibacterial agents, moxifloxacin and rifampicin were individually encapsulated within the pores of mesoporous silica to investigate the efficacy of the nano-delivery system against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Experimental studies revealed that the modification with nanomaterials significantly enhanced the bactericidal effects of both antibiotics against drug-resistant bacteria when used independently, resulting in a marked reduction in the local bacterial load. Moreover, when the two delivery particles were applied concurrently to target and eradicate MRSA, the bacterial mortality rate increased substantially, indicating a potential synergistic effect between the two types of nanoparticles (Aguilera-Correa et al. 2022).

In conclusion, MSNs exhibit significant potential for application in the treatment of infectious diseases. This potential is particularly evident in the context of drug-resistant bacteria, where MSNs can synergistically eradicate pathogens through the incorporation of metal dopants or the delivery of broad-spectrum antibiotics, thereby effectively overcoming bacterial drug resistance barriers and exerting therapeutic effects. Despite the promising nature of the current experimental findings, the majority of studies have been confined to in vitro analyses, lacking comprehensive long-term in vivo investigations necessary to assess the biocompatibility, cytotoxicity, and immunotoxicity of modified MSNs. This limitation poses a substantial obstacle to the clinical translation of MSNs (Xu et al. 2023; Shi et al. 2025; Sha'at et al. 2025). Furthermore, existing short-term in vivo studies have revealed significant cytotoxic and immune-reactive responses, raising concerns regarding the safety profile of MSNs and potentially impeding their clinical application. Consequently, it is imperative to prioritize research focused on the long-term biocompatibility and toxicity of MSNs to address these critical issues (Feng et al. 2022). Simultaneously, owing to the diverse requirements inherent in clinical processes, MSNs frequently necessitate distinct surface modifications. The methodologies and synthesis procedures for these modifications are characterized by numerous techniques and intricate steps. The interaction mechanisms among these processes remain unexplored, potentially leading to the emergence of novel cellular and tissue toxicities, thereby posing risks to patient health (Bindini et al. 2021; Shehata et al. 2025).

Consequently, establishing a standardized and refined procedural framework concerning mesoporous silica and its derivative materials is imperative. This objective and the exploration of additional antibacterial strategies necessitate the collaborative efforts of researchers across multiple disciplines, including chemistry, materials science, and clinical medicine. Such interdisciplinary collaboration will facilitate the translation of MSNs into clinical practice and industrial production, thereby offering more clinical alternatives to address bacterial infections and resistance issues (Feng et al. 2019; Li et al. 2021; Tian et al. 2022).

sec.title: Application of mesoporous silica nanoparticles in tissue engineering
sec.p: Tissue engineering integrates biomedical approaches and requirements with engineering principles to facilitate tissue repair and regeneration, thereby preserving the functional integrity of damaged organs and systems and aiding in patient recovery. Nonetheless, the cytokines necessary for tissue repair are diverse, rendering a single functional tissue scaffold insufficient to address the complexities of various disease states (Kim et al. 2021; Zhao et al. 2024). The rapid advancements in materials science have instilled renewed optimism among researchers in the field of tissue engineering. This progress involves the integration of nanoparticles with diverse morphologies and functions into tissue scaffolds to mimic the normal cellular mechanical environment and enhance the release of appropriate cytokines (Guan et al. 2022; Rial et al. 2022; Abu Elella et al. 2025).

Among these, MSNs have garnered significant attention owing to their excellent biocompatibility and easily modifiable chemical properties. Through surface modification, MSNs can acquire different functional groups, enabling both covalent and non-covalent interactions with tissue scaffolds. Meanwhile, the favorable biocompatibility of the nanomaterials mitigated the inflammatory response in normal cells. Consequently, MSNs exhibit significant potential for application in tissue engineering. In the subsequent section, the author provides a comprehensive overview of the pertinent applications of MSNs in tissue engineering, with a particular focus on orthopedics, to offer novel insights for the fields of tissue engineering and regenerative medicine (Wan et al. 2024).

The regeneration and repair of bones constitute a significant focus within the field of tissue engineering. Although the process of bone injury repair is among the few biomedical processes in the human body capable of achieving complete restoration through the repair of granulation tissue, alterations in bone density and biomechanical properties may occur in the repaired bones. Tissue engineering technologies have the potential to ameliorate these changes, thereby enhancing patient outcomes. As early as the 1970s, researchers such as Carlisle (1970) identified elevated silicon levels in regions of bone formation. Subsequent experiments on animals, including studies where silicon was supplemented as a nutrient in chickens (Carlisle 1976), demonstrated that silicon supplementation facilitated ossification and increased moisture content in cartilage, thereby improving its compressive resistance (Nutrition Classics 1982). These studies suggest that silicon significantly contributes to bone regeneration, thereby establishing a theoretical foundation for the utilization of mesoporous silica materials in bone regeneration applications. For instance, researchers such as Zhao et al. (2023) synthesized cobalt ion-doped mesoporous silica-coated magnetic nanoparticles (Co-MMSNs) and evaluated their biological effects in a mouse model with large bone defects. These findings demonstrated that Co-MMSNs effectively stimulate osteogenesis and facilitate bone repair by promoting granulation tissue formation. This is achieved through the mediation of angiogenic gene expression in bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells, thereby expediting the bone repair process. Consequently, MSNs can effectively reduce complications associated with large bone defects and enhance the osteogenesis rate in distraction osteogenesis surgery. In addition, it is also important to find suitable drug carriers and deliver bioactive factors to the corresponding sites in a targeted manner to stimulate the response of effector cells and promote the production of granulation tissue (Liang et al. 2019; Szewczyk et al. 2020).

The direct combination of bioactive factors with biological scaffolds can lead to unregulated release of these substances in the body, significantly impacting their pharmacokinetic parameters and pharmacological effects (Shi et al. 2023). MSNs have been identified as effective delivery carriers for bioactive factors or drugs, facilitating a synergistic stimulation that promotes tissue repair. Gong et al. (Gong et al. 2024) synthesized mesoporous silica nanoparticles loaded with the flavonoid naringin (CS@MSNs-Naringin), utilizing chitosan as a gating molecule. This nanodelivery system leverages the natural drug naringin to inhibit osteoclast activity and extend its half-life, thereby enhancing the duration and pharmacological efficacy of the drug. This nanodelivery system significantly enhances osteogenesis by inhibiting the phosphorylation of NF-κB and MAPK signaling pathways and reducing osteoclast gene expression levels. Moreover, its pharmacological effect is superior to that of the single drug component naringin, providing a new alternative drug for bone growth and development.

In conclusion, the rapid advancement of tissue engineering over the past decade can be attributed to the interdisciplinary collaboration and exchange among various fields, including materials science and clinical medicine. The integration of nanoparticles into tissue repair processes has introduced new developmental prospects and vitality to the discipline. Notably, MSNs have garnered significant attention owing to their unique physicochemical properties and biomedical functionalities, offering substantial advantages in tissue engineering applications, particularly in facilitating bone regeneration. Nonetheless, challenges remain in the application of MSNs within tissue engineering. For instance, current research on MSNs has primarily focused on their role as sustained-release drug delivery carriers. Presently, the precise modulation of drug release rates from MSNs remains elusive, hindering the optimization of drug delivery by pharmacokinetic parameters. Currently, MSN vectors frequently lack emphasis on the interactions between vectors and cells, vectors and tissues, as well as their temporal dynamics. Furthermore, the in vivo evolution of MSNs remains underexplored. Addressing these gaps necessitates the utilization of multimodal imaging techniques and the advantageous fluorescence sensing properties of MSNs for a comprehensive study (Figure 5) (Polyak et al. 2023; Dong et al. 2024; Lin et al. 2024; Wan et al. 2024).

sec.title: Biological safety and toxicity of mesoporous silica nanoparticles
sec.p: As previously discussed, mesoporous silica materials exhibit significant potential for application in the treatment of malignant tumors, the mitigation of bacterial resistance, and the facilitation of tissue repair. Consequently, there has been increased scholarly interest in exploring the use of mesoporous silica as an innovative drug delivery system in clinical settings (Zhu et al. 2024; Ghosh et al. 2024; Yousefiasl et al. 2025). Comparable systems, such as exosomes (Lai et al. 2022) and liposomes (Large et al. 2021), have already been extensively utilized in clinical practice owing to their excellent drug loading capacities and robust biocompatibility (He et al. 2018). In contrast to exosomes and liposomes, while mesoporous silica also demonstrates commendable drug loading capabilities and its biocompatibility as an individual material is generally considered safe, the development of composite nanomedicine systems with broader biomedical applications often necessitates the modification, processing, and integration of additional functional groups with individual mesoporous silica particles (Yu et al. 2022; Aminoroaya et al. 2022). While these modifications have enhanced the efficacy of composite materials in targeting tumors and bacteria, they also introduce new risks, limitations, and safety concerns (Lérida-Viso et al. 2023) (Supplementary Table 1). For instance, to improve the targeting capabilities of nanomaterials against malignant tumors, modifications to mesoporous silica are necessary. Although these modifications significantly enhance the ability of nanoparticles to target and destroy tumor cells, they also result in the accumulation of nanoparticles in the affected organs, thereby increasing the organ burden and potentially leading to new side effects (Mehravi et al. 2017; Pecoraro et al. 2017; Kim et al. 2022; Liu, et al., 2024). In light of these challenges, this study reviews the cytotoxicity and biodegradability of mesoporous silica-related composites, thereby facilitating their clinical translation and application.

sec.title: Mesoporous silica nanoparticles organization and cytotoxicity
sec.p: Nanotoxicological research indicates that the toxicity of inorganic nanoparticles is frequently associated with variables such as particle size, dosage, duration of exposure, and surface modification (Kumar et al. 2012). Certain inorganic nanoparticles, notably gold nanoparticles, exhibit pronounced toxicity and can induce pathological responses, including inflammation, oxidative stress, and encapsulation by granulation tissue shortly after they enter the body (Ban et al. 2023). In comparison, mesoporous silica has superior biocompatibility relative to that of metal nanoparticles. Consequently, its short-term cytotoxic effects on cells and tissues are less pronounced than those of gold nanoparticles, although it still has some impact (Acosta et al. 2018). Currently, there is a paucity of research on the long-term nanotoxicological effects of nanomaterials, including mesoporous silica. For example, investigations into the impact of nanoparticles on hemodynamics, gastrointestinal motility, and organ accumulation remain limited. This may be related to the current lack of methods, such as monitoring mesoporous silica enrichment. Based on this, a summary of the cellular and tissue toxicity related to mesoporous silica will be prsentated in this section.

MSNs are frequently utilized as nanocarriers for oral drug delivery owing to their ability to modify the pharmacokinetic parameters of drugs, thereby extending their duration of action and enhancing their targeting capabilities, which in turn influences the pharmacological effects of the drugs (Akram et al. 2021). Nonetheless, the oral administration of mesoporous silica particles can result in varying biological toxic effects on cells and tissues, contingent upon differences in particle size and surface modification. For instance, researchers such as Li et al. (2015) investigated the acute toxic reactions associated with the oral administration of MSN particles of three distinct sizes, characterized by length-to-diameter ratios of 1, 1.75, and 5, respectively. The experimental findings revealed that following the administration of 40 mg/kg of each MSN particle size into the gastric system of mice, the MSNs were distributed in the liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys, suggesting effective intestinal absorption of the MSNs.

The differential accumulation of MSNs in various organs suggests that MSNs with a length-to-diameter ratio of 1.5 preferentially accumulate in the liver and lungs, whereas those with a ratio of 1 are predominantly enriched in the spleen. This observation underscores the influence of MSN morphology on their organ-specific distribution. This study further assessed the biological toxicity of three MSN morphologies, revealing significant nephrotoxicity across all types. Notable renal tubular hemorrhage, necrosis, congestion, and edema of renal interstitial vessels were observed. Importantly, the severity of renal injury was closely associated with the length-to-diameter ratio of the MSNs. Specifically, spherical MSNs with a ratio of 1 exhibited the most pronounced renal damage, while elongated rod-shaped MSNs with a ratio of 5 presented the least renal toxicity (Figure 6) (Li et al. 2015; Khedr et al. 2025; Lechuga et al. 2025).

caption.p: Biotoxicity and biodegradability of mesoporous silica nanoparticles. (A) The toxic effects of silica nanoparticles on the spermatogenic epithelium involved hyaline degeneration, cell necrosis, and eosinophilic necrosis. Reprinted with permission from Lechuga et al. (2025). Copyright 2025 Springer Nature. (B) Biodegradation efficiency of mesoporous silica nanoparticles synthesized with different activators. Reprinted with permission from Yu et al. (2012). Copyright 2025 MDPI.

sec.p: In addition to the particle size of MSNs, their surface modification plays a crucial role in influencing the biological toxicity of these nanoparticles. Effective surface modification can enhance the interaction of MSNs with tissues and cells within the circulatory system, thereby mitigating their biological toxicity. For example, researchers such as Liu and colleagues (Liu et al. 2024) have modified MSNs by establishing a concentration gradient of Mg2+ ions to inhibit the formation of siloxanes in nanomaterials. This approach, while preserving the three-dimensional structure of the MSNs, effectively reduces the stability of Si─O─Si bonds, increases the release of Mg and Si ions, and significantly enhances cellular resistance to oxidative stress, ultimately reducing the cellular and tissue toxicity of the MSNs. Similarly, researchers such as Yu et al. (2012) have examined the impact of mesoporous silica geometry and surface modification on its biological toxicity. The findings of their research indicated that the primary source of the biological toxicity of MSNs was the mechanical obstruction of associated blood vessels, leading to multi-organ failure as a result of ischemia. Surface modification of MSNs with amines was shown to significantly enhance their biocompatibility. Specifically, the maximum tolerated dose of amine-modified MSNs was observed to be 100–150 g/kg, in contrast to unmodified MSNs, which exhibited a maximum tolerated dose of only 35–65 g/kg (Yu et al. 2012).

In conclusion, MSNs frequently accumulate in various tissues and organs, influenced by variations in surface modification and particle size. This accumulation can lead to vascular damage, resulting in pathological conditions such as congestion, edema, and hemorrhage in the affected tissues (Mohammadpour et al. 2020; Yang et al. 2023; Zhang et al. 2023). To mitigate the biological toxicity of MSNs, their pore structure, size, and surface characteristics can be optimized. Such modifications can effectively enhance the maximum tolerated dose and improve the biocompatibility of MSNs (Table 2).

caption.p: Summarization of key in vitro and in vivo data.

sec.title: Biodegradability and immunoreactivity of mesoporous silica nanoparticles
sec.p: In the context of translating MSNs into clinical applications, it is imperative to thoroughly examine the interactions between MSNs and the immune system. Following their absorption into the bloodstream via the intestinal tract, MSNs inevitably encounter and interact with adaptive immune cells within the circulatory system (Fortis et al. 2024). The immune system plays a crucial role in maintaining homeostasis within the human body by monitoring and identifying exogenous non-self entities, such as bacteria (Rousseau et al. 2023) and viruses (Lee et al. 2024). They employ adaptive or specific immune mechanisms to phagocytize and eliminate these external agents, thereby preserving the stability of the body's internal environment (Li et al. 2021). As synthetically engineered nanoparticles, MSNs do not undergo the early clonal selection processes necessary for acquiring immune tolerance. Consequently, when the size, particle size, and surface characteristics of MSNs surpass certain thresholds, they are prone to recognition and subsequent attack by the body's immune cells.

Neutrophils, macrophages, and other immune cells secrete substantial quantities of cytokines, potentially initiating an inflammatory response to eliminate MSNs (Pham et al. 2021). Consequently, under typical conditions, researchers often modify the physicochemical properties and surface characteristics of MSNs to mitigate immune system recognition and extend the nanoparticles' circulation time. Conversely, some researchers exploit the inherent immunogenicity of MSNs by designing them as nano-vaccines to elicit a targeted immune response. In such cases, precise control over the biodegradability of MSNs is essential to ensure that excessive inflammatory reactions are avoided while the immune response is stimulated (Nguyen et al. 2020). Thus, the interaction between MSNs and the immune system is contingent upon the specific objectives of the researchers. When effectively harnessed, these interactions can enhance the biomedical efficacy of MSNs. Conversely, neglecting the interaction between MSNs and the immune system may precipitate cytokine storms and inflammatory responses resembling water droplets. The interaction is predominantly influenced by the duration of exposure to mesoporous silica within the circulatory system, which is directly related to the biodegradability of MSNs. In light of this, this section will review the biodegradability of MSNs and the potential adverse immune reactions they may elicit, offering insights for future researchers and designers in the field.

In an ideal scenario, nanomaterials should undergo degradation or excretion at specific sites within the body following the completion of their biomedical functions, as prolonged exposure to nanomaterials can compromise their biosafety and biocompatibility. MSNs, as inorganic nanomaterials, exhibit greater resistance to in vivo degradation compared to organic materials. Consequently, this may result in the accumulation of MSNs in organs and tissues, potentially inducing acute toxic reactions or long-term immunotoxicity due to inflammatory responses. These outcomes are detrimental to the clinical translation of MSNs. To enhance the biodegradability of nanomaterials, their surface area, morphology, and surface modifications must be modulated (Figure 7) (Foster et al. 2019; Liu et al. 2019; Huang et al. 2019; Henderson et al. 2024).

caption.p: Interactions between mesoporous silica nanoparticles and the immune system in vivo. (A) The effects of different sizes of silicon nanoparticles on immune system stimulation and delivery were investigated. Reprinted with permission from Foster et al. (2019). Copyright 2024 American Chemical Society. (B) Silica caused inflammation and granulomas in the lung tissue of the mice. Reprinted with permission from Liu et al. (2019). Copyright 2019 Frontiers. (C) Interaction between dendritic cells and nanoparticles and fluorescence imaging. Reprinted with permission from Huang et al. (2019). Copyright 2019 American Chemical Society. (D) Synthesis and characterization of a novel silica nanoparticle-mediated immunomodulatory adjuvant delivery system. Reprinted with permission from Hao et al. (2012). Copyright 2019 American Chemical Society.

sec.p: For instance, Hao et al. (2012) synthesized MSNs with various shapes and functionalized them with polyethylene glycol. Our findings indicated that spherical MSNs demonstrated superior biodegradability compared to rod-shaped MSNs, which can be attributed to the larger surface area of the spherical MSNs. The study demonstrated that functionalized MSNs undergo more rapid degradation internally, whereas unmodified MSNs experience degradation primarily at their surface. These findings indicate that the physicochemical characteristics and surface modifications of MSNs can significantly extend their degradation time in vivo. This prolonged degradation allows MSNs to interact with plasma proteins and other biomolecules during extended exposure within the circulatory system. Choi et al. (2022) reported that the interaction between MSNs and plasma proteins enhances the immunogenicity of the resulting complex, leading to pronounced ear inflammation. This inflammation results in the accumulation of immune cells in the ears of mice and causes pathological hyperplasia of the epidermis surrounding the ear. Serological analysis of the mice revealed that the MSN-protein complex elevates the levels of histamine, Th1/Th2/Th17 cytokines, and other inflammatory mediators in the bloodstream. This finding indicates that prolonged exposure to MSN may not only cause damage to specific organs and tissues via the enrichment effect, but also facilitate interactions between MSN and other biomolecules within the body, potentially triggering an inflammatory response.

In conclusion, MSNs have garnered significant interest from researchers across various disciplines owing to their exceptional physicochemical properties and extensive potential for biomedical applications. Consequently, numerous application avenues have been explored. These applications frequently necessitate distinct targets and durations of action, making it imperative to regulate the biodegradation capabilities of MSNs to prevent immune system responses triggered by prolonged exposure, which could result in inflammatory reactions. Nonetheless, the regulation of MSN biodegradability remains in its nascent stages, and discrepancies between in vivo and in vitro studies concerning immunoreactivity detection may yield divergent experimental outcomes. Thus, substantial progress is required to ensure the biological safety of MSNs, necessitating collaborative efforts from researchers in diverse fields to mitigate the biotoxicity of MSNs and facilitate their transition into clinical applications (Lee et al. 2013; Kankala et al. 2022) (Figure 8).

caption.p: Schematic diagram of the clinical translation pathway for mesoporous silica nanoparticles.

sec.title: Conclusions and outlook
sec.p: Since the first mesoporous silica nanomaterials were synthesized in 1992, MSNs have attracted wide attention from researchers in many fields because of their good drug loading capacity, well-organized pores, and simple synthesis processes. Researchers from different fields are exploring the application and properties of MSNs in different aspects, among which the related research in biomedicine is the most remarkable. Among them, MSNs have broad application prospects in tumor therapy, drug resistance, tissue engineering, and other fields because of their unique physical and chemical properties and biological effects (Table 3). This paper systematically summarizes the application of MSNs in biomedical fields, especially in the fields of tumor therapy, drug resistance, and tissue engineering, and also summarizes the synthesis and modification of several commonly used MSNs, such as the M41S series and the SBA series. It is believed that this paper will be helpful to readers in the design, synthesis, modification, and biomedical application of MSNs. Although the current achievements of MSNs are surprising, some problems still need to be solved urgently. Compared with those of other drug delivery systems, the synthesis and surface modification of MSNs are simpler and feasible, which provides a premise for the optimization and modification of MSNs. However, the tissue toxicity, cytotoxicity, and biodegradation caused by MSNs in the body are still unresolved problems in the field of MSNs. To solve this problem, a long period of preclinical research is still needed to analyze the enrichment and interaction of MSNs in various organs. Moreover, the same time, the biodegradation of MSNs in different body fluids also needs to be considered. To complete the transformation of MSNs to clinical applications, it is necessary to design a mesoporous silica derivative with no biotoxicity, easy degradation, and significant biomedical effects. This is undoubtedly a long way to go because current biomedical research on MSNs tends to pay more attention to the downstream application performance of MSNs and ignores possible biosafety problems, resulting in a lack of long-term in vivo and in vitro toxicity studies of MSNs.

caption.p: Advantages and disadvantages of mesoporous silica nanoparticles applications.

Promoting angiogenesis and osteogenic differentiation of MSCs
The poor biodegradability of mesoporous silica leads to its long-term accumulation in the body, which may trigger chronic inflammatory responses or impair the growth of new tissues.
Strontium-loaded MSN embedded in hydrogel scaffolds
Excellent porosity, thermal stability, degradability and swelling capacity

In vivo, the pore structure of mesoporous silica may be affected by factors such as hydrolysis, which may lead to the shrinkage or disappearance of the pore size and affect its effectiveness as a scaffold.
Smaller camptothecin dose, no tumor recurrence

Despite its excellent drug-loading capacity, mesoporous silica often exhibits unpredictable release rates during drug delivery, resulting in either premature or incomplete drug release that compromises therapeutic efficacy.
NIR photothermal therapy for 4T1 mice breast cancer
Improvement of the conversion efficiency of solar thermal energy and its long-term stability

To enable targeted drug delivery, mesoporous silica nanoparticles typically require surface modification. However, this process may alter the structure of the drug carrier or reduce its drug-loading capacity. Furthermore, the biocompatibility and biodegradability of the modified materials also need further optimization.

sec.p: After entering the body, nanoparticles inevitably come into contact with various biomolecules and metabolic substances. During this process, interactions may lead to changes in the properties of MSNs. Therefore, it is necessary to perform tracer analysis on MSNs to study their changes in the whole process from entering the body to their successful degradation of MSNs. In terms of drug delivery, although MSNs can effectively improve the stability and targeting ability of loaded drugs, the specific pharmacokinetic parameters remain unclear, and the delivery to targeted organs is not available. These problems affect the further transformation and application of MSNs in clinical practice. The application range of single MSNs remains relatively limited. To improve the biomedical role of nanosystems and expand their application range, it is sometimes necessary to use nanocomposite systems formed from the composites of multiple nanoparticles, which means that it is necessary to introduce one or more different nanoparticles into the original single MSNs. At present, many studies have proved that this composite nanosystem is better than a single MSN, but the process of this composite material is not uniform, which is not conducive to large-scale production. Moreover, the industrial production requirements of nano-carriers ultimately used in clinical practice are not consistent with the production requirements of general goods, which means that the industrial and refined production of MSNs and their derived materials is currently facing difficulties and requires greater manpower and material resources. To date, most MSNs have been used primarily in biomedical research, and there is almost no research on the disease related to biological mechanisms, which makes the mechanism and target of MSNs remain unclear. However, many diseases have been confirmed by clinical medical research to have pathological heterogeneity among different patients. This heterogeneity may cause different interactions between nanoparticles and cells in the body. Therefore, more in-depth studies are required to investigate the molecular changes in lesions in patients after the use of MSNs.

In summary, we summarize the synthesis, modification, and biomedical applications of MSNs and analyze the challenges in the clinical translation of MSNs. The author believes that with the gradual deepening of research methods and continuous innovation of research methods, MSNs can successfully solve related risks and challenges, and successfully realize clinical translation and application.
